title: Boltzmann Distribution from Invariance of Coarse-Graining-Scale and Energy-Shift
authors: Weicheng Fu, Yisen Wang, Yong Zhang, Hong Zhao
abstract: We present a concise derivation of the Boltzmann form for single-particle energy distributions in classical many-body Hamiltonian systems. The derivation relies on two physical facts: coarse-graining-scale invariance of the empirical distribution and invariance under a uniform shift of the energy zero. These conditions uniquely yield the Boltzmann factor, whose parameter is fixed by the mean energy per particle. For separable Hamiltonians, the equilibrium weight factorizes into kinetic and configurational contributions sharing the same parameter, identified from the kinetic part as the inverse kinetic temperature. The principle extends to any physical quantity with a stationary distribution and translational invariance. It is illustrated in a one-dimensional diatomic hard-core gas and a nonlinear lattice chain, where it predicts velocity, energy, spacing, collision-time, and pressure-dependent displacement distributions in agreement with simulations. The lattice model further shows how harmonic elasticity, anharmonic corrections, internal pressure, and thermal expansion emerge from the same exponential equilibrium weights. Finally, the relationships among different ensembles are briefly discussed.
summary: This paper provides a simplified derivation of the Boltzmann distribution by showing it is the unique solution arising from energy-shift and coarse-graining-scale invariance. It demonstrates how complex thermodynamic phenomena, such as thermal expansion and internal pressure, emerge naturally from these fundamental symmetries in 1D models.
main_problem: Deriving the Boltzmann distribution for single-particle energy distributions in classical many-body Hamiltonian systems using a concise, alternative approach based on fundamental symmetries.
main_result: The Boltzmann factor is uniquely determined by the combination of coarse-graining-scale invariance and energy-shift invariance, which leads to the multiplicative Cauchy functional equation.
method: The authors use a mathematical derivation based on functional equations (multiplicative Cauchy equation) and validate the theory using event-driven and Molecular Dynamics (MD) simulations.
model_or_system: The study examines classical many-body Hamiltonian systems, specifically a 1D diatomic hard-core gas and a 1D nonlinear lattice chain with an asymmetric anharmonic potential.
key_observables: Single-particle velocity, energy, and spacing distributions; collision-time intervals; kinetic temperature; internal pressure; and mean relative displacement.
important_parameters: Inverse kinetic temperature (beta), mean energy per particle, mass difference (delta), anharmonic coefficients (alpha, lambda), and thermodynamic limits (N, L to infinity).
main_assumptions: The system is a classical many-body Hamiltonian system in the thermodynamic limit, the distribution function is continuous, and the dynamics are sufficiently mixing/ergodic.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows agreement between theoretical and numerical PDFs for velocity, energy, and collision time in a hard-core gas. Figure 2 shows convergence of kinetic energy and pressure and agreement in PDFs for a nonlinear lattice. Figure 3 shows analytical scaling of pressure and displacement versus temperature.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the derivation principles, applies them to a 1D hard-core gas, extends the framework to nonlinear lattice chains to show emergent properties like thermal expansion, and concludes with a discussion on ensemble equivalence and limitations.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a)–(c) Probability density functions of the particle energy ρ(ε), interparticle spacing ρ(x), and collision time interval ρ(∆t) for different parameter sets. Symbols denote numerical results, while solid lines denote the corresponding theoretical predictions. (d) Mean free time τ as a function of the mass difference δ. All re- sults are obtained for N = 100, m = 1, and ℓ= 1.

Figure 1 compares numerical simulations with theoretical predictions for different parameter settings at fixed system size N = 100, m = 1, and ℓ= 1. FIG. 2. Molecular dynamics results for a lattice chain with peri- odic boundaries, α = λ = 1, N = 100, T = 0.5, and P = −1. (a, b) Time evolution of the average kinetic energy and internal pressure, respectively, showing rapid convergence to the theoretical values (see dashed lines). (c, d) Probability density functions of particle velocity and relative displacement x, respectively, compared with the corre- sponding theoretical curves.

FIG. 3. Temperature dependence of the pressure and mean displacement obtained from Eq. (47). (a) |P| versus T at λ = 1 and l = 0 for different α. (b) |⟨x⟩| versus T at λ = 1 and P = 0 for different α. (c) |⟨x⟩| versus T at λ = 1 and α = −1 for different P. Symbols denote numerical solutions. Solid and dashed lines in (a) and (b) indicate the low- and high-temperature asymptotic predictions, respectively. In (c), the black dashed line marks the stable root x∗of Eq. (55), and the blue solid line gives the high-temperature limit. Insets in (a) and (b) show the corresponding rescaled data.
